agent_output: {"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Bird and domain expert Yao fundamentally disagree on the order model. Bird defines an order as a single, atomic entity with one status where partial fulfillment is invalid and any vendor failure cancels the entire order. Yao defines the correct model as a composite order (basket) containing independent sub-orders per vendor, each with its own status and lifecycle, where a single vendor failure cancels only that vendor's sub-order. These are mutually exclusive domain models that produce opposite system behavior on vendor failure.","agents_involved":["Bird","Yao (external domain expert)"],"options":["Option A (Bird's model): Single atomic order. Simpler to implement but punishes all vendors and the customer when one vendor fails. Appropriate if the business treats multi-vendor orders as indivisible bundles.","Option B (Yao's model): Composite basket with per-vendor sub-orders. Industry-standard marketplace pattern (used by Amazon, Etsy, etc.). More complex but isolates vendor failures. Appropriate if vendors operate independently.","Option C (Hybrid): Single order at payment/customer layer, but internal sub-order tracking per vendor with configurable failure policy (cascade-cancel vs isolate). Defers the business decision to configuration."],"recommendation":"Yao's composite model aligns with established marketplace domain patterns and is almost certainly correct for a multi-vendor marketplace. Bird should review Yao's note and either align or provide a business justification for the atomic model. This must be resolved before any code is written — the entire aggregate design, event model, and fulfillment flow depend on this decision."},{"type":"missing_input","description":"MJ has not yet submitted an architecture summary. Without MJ's technical design (aggregate boundaries, event flows, persistence strategy), a handoff brief to Shaq would lack implementation guidance. The architecture is especially critical here because the Bird-vs-Yao resolution will fundamentally change aggregate design: a single Order aggregate vs a Basket aggregate containing SubOrder aggregates.","agents_involved":["MJ"],"options":["Wait for MJ to complete architecture work after the domain contradiction is resolved","Ask MJ to sketch both architectures (atomic and composite) so Shaq can pivot quickly once Bird and Yao align"],"recommendation":"Resolve the domain contradiction first, then have MJ produce architecture guidance based on the agreed model. Producing architecture before domain alignment would be wasted work."}],"confidence":{"level":30,"high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between Bird and Yao is real and material — not a terminology mismatch but a fundamental disagreement on domain structure","Both positions are internally consistent and well-articulated"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which domain model is correct for this specific business context","Whether there are additional business constraints that would favor Bird's atomic model (e.g., bundled shipping, promotional pricing that requires all items)","Any architectural considerations — MJ has not reported"],"assumptions":["Yao's domain expertise is credible and should carry weight equal to or greater than Bird's analysis on marketplace-specific patterns","MJ's architecture work is a required input for a Shaq handoff, not optional"]}}
